coredata.dc:title: Precise photodynamic therapy: Penetrating the nuclear envelope with photosensitive carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Ning
Du, Jianjun
Yao, Qichao
Ge, Haoying
Li, Haidong
Xu, Feng
Gao, Fengli
Xian, Liman
Fan, Jiangli
Peng, Xiaojun
coredata.dc:description: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has drawn significant attention as a noninvasive cancer treatment. At present, PDT applications are limited in vivo because of the short lifetime and limited diffusion distance of cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the cellular environment. We propose to overcome this challenge by generating limited and short-lived ROS that are localized in key organelles, particularly the nucleus, in tumor cells. We developed Se/N-doped carbon dots (Se/N-CDs) as a photosensitizer for PDT. The Se/N-CDs were shown to bind RNA selectively by digestion experiments, isothermal titration microcalorimetry, and cell dyeing with RNA probes. Se/N-CDs near the nuclear membrane produced ROS that damaged the membrane under light irradiation, permitting the entry of more Se/N-CDs and thereby improving the conversion efficiency of photosensitization. Furthermore, in vitro and in vivo experiments indicated that the Se/N-CDs could inhibit tumor growth more effectively by PDT within the nucleus.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Precise photodynamic therapy
Carbon dots
RNA-Loading
Nucleus penetration
Precise treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell imaging and Se/N-CDs digestion experiment
Structural characterization and spectral properties of Se/N-CDs
Nucleolus imaging in living cells and digestion experiments

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Dispersion of Se/N-CDs in ethanol: (a) transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image, and inset, high-resolution TEM image; (b) dynamic light scattering (DLS); (c) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum; (d) high-resolution XPS of (d) C1s spectra, (e) N1s spectra, and (f) Se3d spectra. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis absorption of Se/N-CDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra of Se/N-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (c) Decreased absorbance of DPBF with Se/N-CDs with increasing irradiation time and (d) linear fit of absorbance at 415 nm with DPBF, methylene blue, and Se/N-CDs under light irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Confocal microscopy imaging of (a) MCF-7 cells incubated with Se/N-CDs and Hoechst 33342 before and after laser irradiation, (b) DNase I and RNase A digestion of incubated Se/N-CDs and Hoechst 33342. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Viability of MCF-7 cells incubated with a series of concentrations of Se/N-CDs before and after yellow LED irradiation for 5 min. (b) Viability of MCF-7 cells incubated with the same concentrations of PpIX. (c) Bright field images of MCF-7 cells with and without Se/N-CDs over time. (d) Confocal microscopy imaging of MCF-7 cells with photo-induced intracellular ROS generation. (e) Confocal microscopy imaging of MCF-7 cells stained with Calcein-AM and propidium iodide before and after PDT treatment. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Tumor growth curves of 4T1 tumor mice in groups (I)–(IV). (b) Relative body weight changes of groups (I)–(IV). (c) Representative pictures of 4T1 tumor mice in groups (I)–(IV). (d) T 2-weighted magnetic resonance imaging for groups (I)–(IV). (e) Hematoxylin-eosin-stained tumor sections from groups (I)–(IV). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic of light-induced entry of nanoparticles larger than nuclear pores into the nucleus. (b) Subcellular co-localization of Se/N-CDs and NBE stain; R is the correlation coefficient of Se/N-CDs and NBE. (c) Confocal microscopy imaging of MCF-7 cells incubated by fluorescence-modified Si nanoparticles (70 nm) before and after light irradiation (relative FL intensity of Si nanoparticles inset, F1/F2 = nuclear fluorescence/cytoplasm fluorescence). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic of photo-induced Se/N-CDs targeting with RNA and enhanced PDT efficiency.

head.ce:title: Precise photodynamic therapy: Penetrating the nuclear envelope with photosensitive carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Research Institute, Dalian University of Technology, Nanshan District, Shenzhen, 518057, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenzhen Research Institute, Dalian University of Technology, Nanshan District, Shenzhen, 518057, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116024, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has drawn significant attention as a noninvasive cancer treatment. At present, PDT applications are limited in vivo because of the short lifetime and limited diffusion distance of cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the cellular environment. We propose to overcome this challenge by generating limited and short-lived ROS that are localized in key organelles, particularly the nucleus, in tumor cells. We developed Se/N-doped carbon dots (Se/N-CDs) as a photosensitizer for PDT. The Se/N-CDs were shown to bind RNA selectively by digestion experiments, isothermal titration microcalorimetry, and cell dyeing with RNA probes. Se/N-CDs near the nuclear membrane produced ROS that damaged the membrane under light irradiation, permitting the entry of more Se/N-CDs and thereby improving the conversion efficiency of photosensitization. Furthermore, in vitro and in vivo experiments indicated that the Se/N-CDs could inhibit tumor growth more effectively by PDT within the nucleus.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is an effective and clinically approved treatment for eliminating tumors without invasive surgery, typically by killing tumor cells using reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as 1O2, generated by a photosensitizer under light irradiation [1–3]. Because the ROS yield usually depends on the efficiency of the photosensitizer, most studies have primarily focused on screening high-efficiency photosensitizers [4]. However, efficient photosensitizers consume O2 very fast, which aggravates the hypoxic environment in which solid tumors thrive, such that PDT becomes unsustainable [5,6]. Many strategies have been attempted to overcome this limitation by increasing the O2 content directly through physical carriers and/or chemical reactions [7,8]. However, in cancer cells, the lifetime of ROS produced by a photosensitizer is dramatically restricted (to the nanosecond level), and therefore their effective distance is only tens of nanometers [9]. As a result, most of the ROS become deactivated before killing cancer cells, and thus the increased O2 has little impact on the efficacy of the treatment [10–12]. In this context, maximizing the utility of the limited 1O2 available in the most effective location could be the most promising strategy for converting the high efficiency of a photosensitizer in vitro into tumor-killing capacity in vivo.

To improve the anti-tumor efficiency of 1O2 in vivo, the photosensitizer should reach as close to the nucleus of a tumor cell as possible because the nucleus contains the DNA required for cell division, and a photosensitizer that reaches the nucleus would exhibit unprecedented tumor-destroying efficiency. Therefore, strategies that involve fine-tuning the position of the photosensitizer in key locations have been developed as “precision medicine” for subcellular-level tumor treatments [13], including with a mitochondria-targeting photosensitizer [14] and a mitochondrial DNA-targeting Ru-complex [15]. However, photosensitizers, which typically consist of organic molecules and metal complexes, can scarcely penetrate the nuclei of living cells because of the protective nuclear membrane. Therefore, a new strategy or agent is required to maximize photodynamic efficiency and overcome this barrier.

In previous research works, carbon dots (CDs) have been used as photosensitizer carriers to improve PDT performance because of their water solubility, biocompatibility, and penetration capability [16–19], and a few types of carbon dots were found to be effective at generating ROS [20–23]. For example, Wang et al. reported a red-emitting graphene quantum dot (GQD) photosensitizer that generated high counts of singlet oxygen species [20]. Although several carbon dot types have been demonstrated to be effective at improving PDT efficiency, such as organelle-specific graphene quantum dots in mitochondria [24], the ability to kill cancer cells with precision nucleus-targeting PDT remains a challenge.

To get close to the negatively charged nuclear envelop, N-doped positive carbon dots with rigid structures are preferable, and the heavy atom effect in organic photosensitizers motivated an investigation into selenium to enhance the probability of intersystem crossing to increase PDT efficiency [25]. Therefore, inspired by the Se/N doping strategy, we developed highly effective Se/N carbon dots (Se/N-CDs) from bis(2,3,6,7-trtrahydro-1H, 5H-pyrido [3,2,1-ij] quinolin-8yl)selane (BTPS). These Se/N-CDs are herein shown to be capable of binding RNA by experiments such as digestion, isothermal titration microcalorimetry, and cell dyeing with RNA probes. RNA is proposed to act as an effective carrier for transporting the photosensitizer (Se/N-CDs) near to the nucleus, resulting in disruption of the nuclear membrane under light irradiation. Following nuclear penetration, Se/N-CDs are shown to be highly effective at killing cancer cells in vivo, even though Se/N-CDs demonstrated only 10.6% 1O2-generating efficiency, which is much lower than commercial photosensitizers (methylene blue (MB), porphyrin derivatives, and so forth).

1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were recorded on a Varian INOVA-400 or a Bruker Avance II 400 MHz/500 MHz spectrometer. Chemical shifts (δ) were reported as ppm in deuterated chloroform (CDCl3) with tetramethylsilane as the internal standard. Fluorescence spectra were acquired with an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer, and absorption spectra were measured on an Agilent Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer G6860A. The double-distilled water used in all experiments was from Milli-Q systems. Transmission electron microscopy was performed with 300 KV Transmission Electron Microscope (TF30) and 120 KV Transmission Electron Microscope, and the MTT assay was analyzed on a Thermo Fisher Spectrophotometer 3020. An Olympus FV1000 was used for microscope imaging. The magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) were performed on a 0.5 T MesoMR23-060H–I.

BTPS (40 mg) was dissolved in 10 mL of anhydrous ethanol and then transferred to a Teflon autoclave. The mixture was heated at 200 °C for 10 h. The reaction solution was centrifuged at 12 000 r min−1 for 15 min to remove larger particles, and then the upper liquid was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to obtain a red liquid. Finally, the Se/N-CDs were freeze-dried to obtain a dark brown powder. As showed in Figs. S20 and S21, the NMR data of BTPS was correct.

1, 3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), as a singlet oxygen trapping agent, was used to determine the singlet oxygen quantum yield of the Se/N-CDs, using MB as reference. A mixture of DPBF and Se/N-CDs was irradiated by an LED source, and the absorption spectra were recorded with a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The singlet oxygen quantum yield was calculated according to the following equation:
Φ △ = Φ MB (k CD × F MB) / (k MB × F CD) × 100%
Where Φ △ is the singlet oxygen quantum yield of Se/N-CDs, Φ MB is the singlet oxygen quantum yield of methylene blue (Φ MB = 57% in DCM), k is the decomposition rate of DPBF, and F is the correction factor applied to the absorbance spectra of the test compound and reference compound (F = 1 − 10−OD ), where OD is the UV absorption value of the respective compounds at the irradiation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging and Se/N-CDs digestion experiment
ce:section.ce:para: Human breast cancer cells (MCF-7), human epithelial carcinoma cells (HeLa), human hepatoma cell (HepG2) and breast cancer cells from mouse (4T1) were cultured in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin-streptomycin. The cells were seeded in a Petri dish and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in a 5% CO2 environment.

The MCF-7 cells and the HeLa cells were stained with Se/N-CDs (5 μg mL−1) and Hoechst 33342 (2.5 μg mL−1) for 15 min in the incubator, and then washed twice with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) before imaging. An Olympus FV-1000 inverted fluorescence microscope with a 60 × objective lens was used for cell imaging.

Additional MCF-7 cells were cultured and incubated in a Petri dish with DMEM, 10% fetal bovine serum, and 1% penicillin-streptomycin for use in the digestion experiments. These cells were also fixed by ice ethanol for 20 min at −20 °C and then washed twice with PBS, prior to digestion by ribonuclease A (RNase A) and deoxyribonuclease I (DNase I) solution at 37 °C for 3 h. The pre-treated cells were washed twice with PBS before staining by Se/N-CDs and Hoechst 33342, and the stained cells were imaged by confocal fluorescent imaging.

The cytotoxicity and phototoxicity of Se/N-CDs were tested using 3-(4,5)-dimethylthiahiazo (-2-yl)-3,5-diphenytetrazoliumromide (MTT). MCF-7 cells were seeded in 96-well microplates (Nunc, Denmark), at a density of 1 × 105 cells mL−1 in 100 μL DMEM containing 10% fetal bovine serum, for 24 h at 37 °C, and then washed with 100 μL of PBS. Se/N-CDs were diluted to different concentrations of 0, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 5.0 and 7.5 μg mL−1 for cell incubation (15 min, 37 °C, 5% CO2). The cells were irradiated using a yellow LED (550 nm, 10 mW cm−2, 5 and 10 min durations). Additional cells were cultured and treated with the same Se/N-CDs concentrations without LED irradiation.

All cell samples were further incubated in DMEM with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin-streptomycin for 12 h. The cells were then washed twice with PBS prior to adding 100 μL of diluted MTT solution (0.5 mg mL−1) into each well and culturing at 37 °C for 4 h in 5% CO2. The final cell media were carefully cleaned, leaving crystals of formazan that were then dissolved by adding 100 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide. The optical density was determined on a microplate reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific), with subtraction of the absorbance of the cell-free blank volume at 490 nm. A commercial Protoporphyrin IX (PpIX) volume (630 nm, 10 mW cm−2 for 5 min) was tested under same experimental conditions. The cell viability was calculated according to the following equation:
Cell viability (%) = (ODCD − ODKCD) / (ODcontrol − ODKcontrol) × 100
Where ODCD refers to the absorbance of the Se/N-CDs, ODKCD refers to the absorbance of the cell-free blank volume, ODcontrol refers to the absorbance of the blank control hole, and ODKcontrol refers to the absorbance of the blank control hole in the cell-free blank volume.

Tumor-bearing BALB/c mice (4–6 weeks old) were purchased from the SPF Experimental Animal Center at Dalian Medical University. The study was conducted in accordance with the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals published by the United States National Institutes of Health (8th edition, 2011), and the animal protocol was approved by the review board of the Animal Ethics Committee of Dalian University of Technology (Certificate of Approval number 2018–043). A NightOWL II LB983 small animal in vivo imaging system equipped with a sensitive charge coupled device (CCD) camera was used for the bioimaging experiments, with excitation at 550 nm and a 620 nm emission filter. The 4T1 cells were injected into the left axilla of female BALB/c mice (20–25 g) to establish the tumor model. After growing for a week, the tumor volume reached approximately 200 mm3, at which point these mice were divided into four groups: two groups received intratumoral injections of 100 μL Se/N-CDs (2.5 mg kg−1 body weight), and the other two groups received intratumoral injections of 100 μL PBS. After the injection for 1 h, two groups (one from each treatment regimen) were irradiated for 20 min with a 550 nm LED (50 mW), these irradiation groups are referred to as PBS+550 nm LED and Se/N-CDs+550 nm LED, respectively. The tumor size and body weight of each mouse were recorded every two days. The tumor volume was calculated according to the following equation: V = ab 2/2, where a and b are the length and width of the tumor, respectively. T2-weighted magnetic resonance imaging (T2-MRI) was performed in mice after 15 days of treatment. The tumor size in mice could be directly observed through T2-MRI to confirm the photodynamic therapy of Se/N-CDs. The mice were sacrificed 15 days after the treatments, and the tumors and main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) were fixed in buffered formalin, embedded in paraffin, sectioned, and stained with hematoxylin and eosin for pathological analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization and spectral properties of Se/N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Se/N-CDs were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method, using BTPS (Scheme S1) as a carbon source in ethanol solution (200 °C, 10 h), and the Se/N-CDs yield was 75%. As shown in Figs. 1a, 1b, and S1, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) revealed the uniform dispersion of Se/N-CDs in ethanol, with an average diameter of 3.6 ± 0.6 nm. No significant grain lattice fringe was observed in high resolution TEM (Fig. 1 a inset), indicating an amorphous carbon [26]. Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns showed that Se/N-CDs exhibited a broad peak (2θ = 22.3°) in Fig. S2, which was consistent with TEM results [27,28]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results indicated that the elemental constituents of the Se/N-CDs were carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and selenium. The typical peaks of C1s, N1s, O1s, and Se3d were detected at approximately 284.4, 398.9, 532, and 55.6 eV, with relative atomic percentages of 74.07, 2.41, 22.04, and 1.48%, respectively (Fig. 1c). The high-resolution spectrum of C1s could be divided into four main peaks at 284.3, 285.5, 286.1, and 288.0 eV, which correspond to C–C/CC, C–N, C–O, and CO, respectively (Fig. 1d) [29]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 1e) revealed two main peaks at 399.0 and 399.9 eV, which correspond to pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N [30]. The O1s spectrum revealed the presence of C–OH (531.9 eV) and CO (532.7 eV), shown in Fig. S3 [31]. The high-resolution spectrum of Se3d, in Fig. 1f, showed two different peaks, corresponding to the signals of C–Se (55.1 eV) and C–Se–Se (55.8 eV) [32].

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of the carbon dots is shown in Fig. S4. The broad peak at 3416 cm−1 corresponds to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations [33], and the peak at 2926 cm−1 was attributed to a C–C stretching vibration [34,35]. The peak from 1582 cm−1–1745 cm−1 corresponds to aromatic CC vibrations and the stretching vibration of carboxyl groups at 1737 cm−1 [36,37]. Other peaks ranging from 1388 cm−1–795 cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibrations of C–N, C–O–C, and C–Se. The above FT-IR results indicated the presence of functional groups such as –COOH, –OH, and –NH2. As expected, by introducing N atoms in the carbon dots, a positive signal of zeta potential (+32 mV) was obtained because of the N doping in ethanol (Fig. S5).

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Se/N-CDs is shown in Fig. 2 a. The peaks at 317 and 550 nm correspond to the n→ π* transition of π systems containing C–N or CO structures, respectively [38]. In ethanol, the emission peak red-shifted from 576 nm to 607 nm when Se/N-CDs were excited at 510–570 nm (Fig. 2b), whereas in aqueous water, emissions localized at approximately 591 nm in the same excitation range, as shown in Fig. S6 [39]. In addition, the Se/N-CDs showed favorable dispersibility and stability in water (Fig. S7), and the Se/N-CDs proved stable for at least 80 min in the cell culture medium, which is important for PDT (Fig. S8). The lifetime of the Se/N-CDs was dominated by double exponential decay at 3.94 ns (70.54%) and 0.79 ns (29.46%), and the weighted average lifetime was approximately 3.70 ns (Fig. S9).

In order to study the photodynamic properties of Se/N-CDs, 1, 3-diphenlisobenzofuran (DPBF) was used as 1O2 capture agent. By reacting with 1O2, DPBF could be irreversible oxidized to 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene, resulting in rapidly decrease of absorbance intensity at 415 nm (Scheme S2) [40–44]. When Se/N-CDs were irradiated by the 550 nm LED source in DCM for 0–360 s, the absorption peak of the DPBF agent at 415 nm was distinctly decreased with increased exposure time as shown in Fig. 2c. The singlet oxygen quantum yield of the Se/N-CDs reached 10.6% (Fig. 2d), which was calculated using methylene blue as reference (Fig. S10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nucleolus imaging in living cells and digestion experiments
ce:section.ce:para: To study the localizing behavior of Se/N-CDs in tumor cells, fluorescence imaging of the MCF-7 cells was performed. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the MCF-7 cells exhibited intense fluorescence signals after only 15 min of incubation with the Se/N-CDs, indicating their favorable biocompatibility and penetration. Analyses of the fluorescence images showed that the Se/N-CDs were mainly located in the cytoplasm, distinguished from the nucleus by Hoechst 33342 staining, a commercial nucleus dye. With light irradiation for several tens of seconds, the nucleoli became illuminated in the red channel and, simultaneously, the fluorescence in the cytoplasm decreased. By comparing the merged fluorescence of Hoechst 33342 with Se/N-CDs in the nuclei, Se/N-CDs were more exactly stained in the nucleoli. The condition of Se/N-CDs entering the cell was observed without light irradiation over a prolonged incubation time. As shown in Fig. S11, Se/N-CDs did not enter the nucleus at different times in the absence of light irradiation. HeLa and 4T1 cell lines were also imaged to confirm this behavior under the same experimental conditions as shown in Fig. S12.

In addition to DNA, most RNA concentrates in the nucleolus for the synthesis of ribosomal RNA (rRNA) and the formation of ribosomes [9]. Therefore, the MCF-7 cells were used in a digestion experiment with RNase A and DNase I, respectively, with Hoechst 33342 as a reference. As shown in Fig. 3b, red fluorescence was observed in nucleoli by Se/N-CDs. When MCF-7 cells were digested by RNase A, the red fluorescence in the nucleoli almost disappeared, whereas the Hoechst 33342 staining results were almost unaffected. In contrast, the Se/N-CDs staining results were not significantly affected after digestion treatment with DNase I. These results confirmed that the Se/N-CDs had a highly selective binding affinity for RNA rather than DNA in cell culture. Furthermore, we also used isothermal titration microcalorimetry to study the binding tendency of Se/N-CDs with RNA and DNA. Under the same conditions, each drop of RNA and DNA absorbed 27.39 μJ and 34.49 μJ, respectively, when binding Se/N-CDs. Moreover, the saturation time of Se/N-CDs with RNA was shorter than that with DNA, meaning complexes of Se/N-CDs with RNA were more feasible and stable than that with DNA (Fig. S13), and we tested the reaction of Se/N-CDs on yeast RNA in PBS and found that the fluorescence of Se/N-CDs decreased with the addition of RNA, indicating that Se/N-CD does target RNA by electrostatic interaction in Fig. S14.

To assess the PDT effect obtained with the Se/N-CDs, the relative viability of the cells was assessed by a standard methyl thiazolyltetrazolium (MTT) assay. First, MCF-7 cells were incubated with different concentrations of Se/N-CDs (0–7.5 μg mL−1) in the absence of light irradiation. After 12 h of incubation, over 90% cell viability was reached, indicating negligible cytotoxicity in the absence of light (Fig. 4 a). In contrast, in the presence of yellow LED irradiation (550 nm, 10 mW cm−2, 5 min), the MCF-7 cell survival rate was less than 20% with a Se/N-CDs concentration of 5.0 μg mL−1. The photodynamic effect of the Se/N-CDs on the MCF-7 cells was also verified by observing changes in the cell morphology in a bright field using laser confocal microscopy. There were very few distinct morphological changes when cells without Se/N-CDs were irradiated by laser. In contrast, when the experimental group containing Se/N-CDs was irradiated, the MCF-7 cells clearly swelled until the cell membrane ruptured, resulting in the release of the cell contents as shown in Fig. 4c.

Thus, the phototoxicity effect exhibited a clear dependency on both the photosensitizer and light irradiation doses as shown in Figs. 4a and S15. PpIX, a commercial photosensitizer, was utilized as a reference. With 2.5 μg mL−1 PpIX, the survival rates of the MCF-7 cells were 76% and 45% in the absence and the presence of light irradiation (630 nm, 10 mW cm−2, 5 min), respectively. Increasing the PpIX concentration to 7.5 μg mL−1, the cell survival rate significantly decreased to 46% in the absence of light; however, the cellular toxicity induced (48%) was unacceptable for use in living organisms. When 7.5 μg mL−1 of Se/N-CDs were introduced, the survival rates of the MCF-7 cells were 91% in the absence of light irradiation and only 10% in the presence of light irradiation. Therefore, the Se/N-CDs proposed in this work exhibited a much better PDT effect and dramatically lower toxicity than PpIX under the same photosensitizer concentration and light dose, as shown in Fig. 4b. In addition, the MTT results from HeLa, HepG2, and 4T1 cell lines also demonstrated the favorable photodynamic effect of Se/N-CDs (Fig. S16).

In order to prove that 1O2 was produced by Se/N-CDs in the cellular microenvironment, 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was added as an intracellular indicator. As shown in Fig. 4d, the MCF-7 cells incubated with Se/N-CDs generated green fluorescence after LED irradiation (550 nm) because of the formation of dichlorolfuorescein oxidized by 1O2. In the control group, a weak fluorescence signal was found in the presence of NaN3 (a type of reactive oxygen scavenger). Finally, Calcein-AM and propidium iodide staining were used to further confirm the therapeutic efficacy of Se/N-CDs. As showed in Fig. 4e, Se/N-CDs caused severe photodynamic damage to cancer cells, and almost all cells were killed after LED irradiation, as indicated by the change in red fluorescence. Cell imaging in the control group showed green fluorescence, indicating that Se/N-CDs did not cause death of cancer cells in the absence of irradiation. The above experimental results indicated that Se/N-CDs were highly capable of photodynamically killing cancer cells.

To study the photodynamic properties of Se/N-CDs in vivo, BALB/c tumor-bearing mice were selected as an animal model. As shown in Fig. S17, before injection of the Se/N-CDs, a weak background fluorescence of tumor tissue was observed by in vivo imaging (NightOWL II LB983). When the Se/N-CDs solution (2.5 mg kg−1 of body weight) was injected intratumorally, the fluorescence was significantly enhanced compared with other parts of the body. To evaluate the PDT effect of Se/N-CDs, the tumor-bearing mice were divided into four groups: (I) injection of PBS; (II) injection of PBS with LED irradiation (550 nm, 50 mW cm−2, 20 min); (III) injection of Se/N-CDs; and (IV) injection of Se/N-CDs with LED irradiation (550 nm, 50 mW cm−2, 20 min). The tumor volume and body weight of the mice in the four groups were measured and recorded every two days. No significant weight loss was observed in any of the groups during the treatment process, indicating a low biological toxicity of the Se/N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the tumor size increased significantly with prolonged time for mice in groups: (I) PBS, (II) PBS+550 nm LED, and (III) Se/N-CDs. However, the tumors were significantly inhibited in the presence of Se/N-CDs under LED irradiation (550 nm, 50 mW cm−2, 20 min), which was also demonstrated through T 2-weighted magnetic resonance imaging as shown in Fig. 5d.

In addition, the isolated tumors of these four groups were separated and measured individually. The tumor volume in group (IV) was significantly smaller than that of each of the other three groups (Fig. 5c), which indicated the therapeutic capability of Se/N-CDs in PDT. Furthermore, hematoxylin-eosin staining of tumor slices clearly showed that tumor tissue was destroyed after treatment with Se/N-CDs+550 nm LED, whereas no obvious tumor necrosis was observed in any of the other three groups, as shown in Fig. 5e. To further analyze the biocompatibility of the Se/N-CDs, the main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) of the mice were stained. Imaging of these organs showed little detectable pathological damage, indicating the excellent biocompatibility and suitability of Se/N-CDs for cancer treatment (Fig. S18).

We hypothesized that Se/N-CDs kill cells in four steps via nucleus-targeting PDT: (I) loading photosensitizers (i.e., Se/N-CDs): Se/N-CDs bind RNA after penetrating cancer cells; (II) locating and triggering the photosensitizers: Se/N-CDs/RNA complexes approach the nuclear membrane automatically and produce ROS under light irradiation; (III) destroying the nuclear membrane to permit entry of more Se/N-CDs; and (IV) killing cancer cells by efficient PDT inside nucleus (Fig. 6 a). RNA is mainly found in the cytoplasm and nucleus. The dye NBE was developed by our group, and this dye proved capable of binding RNA selectively, with a Pearson sample correlation factor (R) of 0.913 compared with SYTO RNA Select™ [45]. In this work, the Se/N-CDs showed an R of 0.940 compared to NBE, suggesting the binding of Se/N-CDs with RNA (Fig. 6b).

Light irradiation is a critical trigger causing Se/N-CDs penetrate nuclear membrane in process III. When the MCF-7 cells incubated with Se/N-CDs and propidium iodide were irradiated for 1 min, the red fluorescence signal of propidium iodide was gradually enhanced as the incubation time increased. However, in the absence of light irradiation, no fluorescence signal was observed even after 1 h of incubation (Fig. S19). Combining the above results, we postulated that ROS generated by Se/N-CDs under light damaged the nuclear membrane, thereby opening the gate of the nuclear envelope.

Furthermore, fluorescent Si nanoparticles with diameters of 70 nm were used as a reference to investigate the formation of large holes in the nuclear membrane. Because Si nanoparticles are much larger than the fenestra (approximately 39 nm), Si nanoparticles typically have difficulty penetrating the nuclear membrane [46]. As shown in Fig. 6c, the Si nanoparticles were only distributed in the cytoplasm before light irradiation with a fluorescence intensity ratio of F
1
/F
2 = 0.11 (where F
1 and F
2 are the fluorescence in the nucleus and the cytoplasm, respectively). However, with light irradiation, the green fluorescence of Si nanoparticles was observed within the nucleus, and the fluorescence in the nucleus became enhanced at a similar intensity to that in the cytoplasm (F
1
/F
2 = 0.97), meaning that holes of more than 39 nm had been established in the nuclear envelope, permitting the entry of more Se/N-CDs [47].

In summary, the proposed Se/N-CDs, which were developed by a one-step solvothermal method, served as a biocompatible and efficient photosensitizer that can be used in PDT. The Se/N-CDs could bind with RNA and were thus transported near the nucleus, subsequently penetrating the nucleus through light-triggered damage to the nuclear membrane. Although the 1O2-generating efficiency (10.6%) was lower than commercial photosensitizers, we obtained an excellent PDT effect because of the unique nuclear targeting, and the proposed approach thus offers significant potential as a reference for next-generation photosensitizers in precision cancer treatments.

This work was finished through contributions of all authors. The design and synthesis of carbon dots used in this work, most of tests in vivo and in vitro were contributed by Mr. N. Xu. The carbon source for synthesizing carbon dots was provided by Mr. Q.C. Yao. Dr. H.D. Li was in charge of cell culture in experiment. Mr. F. Xu and Mr. N. Xu cultured BALB/c mice together to ensure the successful completion of the vivo experiment. With the help of Dr F.L. Gao, the DNA and RNA digestion experiments in the cells were successfully completed. The ToC and Scheme 1 , and TEM tests were completed by Miss H.Y. Ge, and Miss L.M. Xian gave supports in XPS tests and calculation of singlet oxygen efficiency in different situation. Prof. J.L. Fan gave very important discussion and help on how to improve experiments, such as how to prove the binding behavior of CDs with RNA, nucleus penetration in living cells, and so forth. Prof. J.J. Du and Prof. X.J. Peng directed the whole process in this carbon dots system and revised manuscript. All authors have given approval to the final version of this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21421005, 21576037, 21676047 and 21822804). We specially acknowledge Dr. Su Chen, Professor of Nanjing Tech University, for his significance help in CDs moiety of the study.
